attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with increasing fatigue over three months. She reports no other symptoms but mentions her clothes have become looser. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She works as a librarian and lives with her spouse. She has never smoked.

On examination, she appears pale but well. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Abdominal examination reveals no masses or tenderness. Digital rectal examination reveals dark stool. Investigations reveal: haemoglobin 92 g/L (115-165), mean cell volume 76 fL (80-96), ferritin 8 µg/L (30-200), iron 4 µmol/L (10-30). Faecal occult blood test is positive.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with iron deficiency anaemia and positive faecal occult blood, raising concern for bowel cancer. The combination of unexplained iron deficiency anaemia, weight loss, and positive faecal occult blood in an older adult requires prompt investigation to exclude malignancy.

Colonoscopy is the gold standard investigation as it allows direct visualisation of the bowel mucosa and enables tissue biopsy if lesions are identified. It has superior sensitivity and specificity compared to other investigations and provides both diagnostic and potentially therapeutic capabilities.

Gastroscopy would be appropriate if there were upper gastrointestinal symptoms such as dyspepsia, early satiety, or epigastric pain, which are not present in this case. While upper gastrointestinal malignancy can cause iron deficiency anaemia, the presence of dark stools suggests a lower gastrointestinal source.

CT colonography is an alternative when colonoscopy is contraindicated or unsuccessful. However, it does not allow for tissue sampling and has lower sensitivity for small lesions. It would be a second-line investigation in this scenario.

Capsule endoscopy is primarily used to investigate small bowel pathology and is not the appropriate first-line investigation for suspected bowel cancer. It is typically reserved for cases where conventional endoscopy has failed to identify a source of bleeding.

Iron studies have already demonstrated iron deficiency, and further testing would not help identify the underlying cause. The priority is to investigate the cause of the iron deficiency rather than further characterise it.

If colonoscopy confirms bowel cancer, the next step would be staging with CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis to assess for metastatic disease, followed by multidisciplinary team discussion regarding treatment options.
learningPoints: Iron deficiency anaemia in older adults should prompt investigation for bowel cancer.
Colonoscopy is the gold standard investigation for suspected bowel cancer.
CT staging of the chest, abdomen and pelvis is essential in newly diagnosed bowel cancer.
references: Eng C, Yoshino T, Ruíz-García E, Mostafa N, Cann CG, O’Brian B, et al. Colorectal cancer. The Lancet. 2024 Jul 20;404(10449):294–310.
